skill.name: fun-brainstorming
skill.description: Invoke before any creative or architectural work — feature design, component creation, or behavioral changes. A streamlined brainstorming process optimized for fast, focused decision-making.
skill.summary: A structured brainstorming skill that guides from idea to actionable direction quickly, exploring intent and trade-offs without process overhead.
skill.author: roin-orca

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged external_commands and weak cryptographic algorithms, but evaluation reveals these are false positives. The 'external_commands' detection refers to a documented bash command in markdown (not executed code) and a Graphviz diagram block. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detection is a scanner bug - no cryptographic code exists in this file. This is a pure documentation file containing brainstorming methodology with no security risks.

content.value_statement: Get from idea to actionable direction quickly with a lightweight brainstorming process that explores intent and evaluates trade-offs without unnecessary overhead.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through a structured discovery phase to clarify intent and constraints
Present 2-3 approach options with clear trade-offs and recommendations
Facilitate convergent decision-making with explicit user approval
Capture decisions inline or in chat without requiring formal documentation
Adapt depth and formality to match task complexity
Prevent premature implementation by enforcing approval before coding
limitations: Does not execute code or create files - acts only as a thinking partner
No access to external tools or APIs for research
Cannot generate diagrams or visual artifacts
Relies on user input for project-specific context and constraints

use_cases.title: Feature Design Decisions
use_cases.description: When planning a new feature, use this skill to explore options, evaluate trade-offs, and get alignment on direction before writing code.

use_cases.title: Architectural Choices
use_cases.description: Before refactoring or designing new system components, brainstorm the approach to ensure decisions are well-considered.

use_cases.title: Quick Problem Solving
use_cases.description: When facing a technical problem, use the structured approach to systematically explore solutions rather than jumping to the first idea.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Brainstorming Request
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to brainstorm [describe your task or problem]. Can we explore some approaches together?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new feature or facing a technical decision

prompt_templates.title: Trade-off Exploration
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm considering [option A] vs [option B] for [use case]. What are the trade-offs of each approach, and which would you recommend?

prompt_templates.title: Decision Review
prompt_templates.prompt: I've been thinking about [problem] and my initial idea is [describe idea]. Can you help me think through whether this is the right direction?
prompt_templates.scenario: Validating a initial idea before proceeding

prompt_templates.title: Constraint-Aware Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to solve [problem] but I have these constraints: [list constraints]. What approaches would work within these limits?
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning with specific constraints or requirements

output_examples.input: I need to add user authentication to my web app. Should I use a third-party service or build it myself?
output_examples.output:
Great question! Let me start by understanding your context better.

**Discover:**
- What type of web app is this? (SaaS, internal tool, public website)
- Do you need social logins, email/password, or both?
- What's your user base size?

While you answer those, here are two initial directions to consider:

**Option A: Third-party auth (Auth0, Clerk, Supabase Auth)**
- Pros: Fast setup, maintained security, built-in features
- Cons: Vendor lock-in, ongoing subscription costs, less control

**Option B: Build your own (with libraries like NextAuth, Lucia)**
- Pros: Full control, no vendor dependency, can customize deeply
- Cons: More implementation time, security burden, ongoing maintenance

My recommendation: For most web apps, start with a third-party service unless you have specific customization needs. The time saved on security and maintenance usually outweighs the costs.

What are your priorities?

output_examples.input: We're building a real-time notification system. Should we use WebSockets or Server-Sent Events?
output_examples.output:
Good architectural question. Let me explore your needs.

**Discover:**
- Do you need bidirectional communication (client can also send to server) or just server-to-client?
- How many concurrent users are you planning for?
- Will notifications work across multiple devices per user?

**Options to consider:**

**WebSockets**
- Full-duplex bidirectional communication
- Slightly more complex to scale
- Better for chat apps, collaborative features

**Server-Sent Events (SSE)**
- One-way server-to-client
- Simpler, works over HTTP, auto-reconnect
- Perfect for notifications, live updates

**Recommendation:** If you only need server-to-client notifications, start with SSE. It's simpler to implement and scale. Use WebSockets only if clients need to send messages back through the same connection.

best_practices: Start with the Discover phase to clarify intent before jumping to solutions
Present options with clear trade-offs, and lead with your recommendation
Get explicit user approval before moving to implementation
Batch your clarifying questions into one message to respect user time
Match the depth of discussion to the complexity of the task
anti_patterns: Jumping straight to code without brainstorming approval
Asking one question at a time instead of batching
Presenting more than 3 options which overwhelms rather than helps
Skipping the approval step and assuming user agreement
Using formal documentation for simple decisions when chat suffices

faq.question: When should I invoke this skill?
faq.answer: Invoke this skill before any feature design, architectural decision, or implementation task. It helps ensure you've thought through the problem before writing code.

faq.question: Can I skip the brainstorming process for small tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends proportional depth. Small bug fixes or config changes might skip directly to proposing. Use your judgment based on task complexity.

faq.question: What if the user rejects my proposal?
faq.answer: Revise and repropose up to 2 times. If still not aligned after that, ask the user to state their preference directly rather than continuing to iterate.

faq.question: Do I need to create formal documentation?
faq.answer: No. The skill emphasizes capturing decisions inline in code or in chat. Only create separate documents if the user specifically requests it.

faq.question: How do I handle technical constraints I'm unfamiliar with?
faq.answer: Be honest about what you don't know. Ask the user about their constraints, research together, or suggest involving someone with domain expertise.

faq.question: Can this skill help with non-technical decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. The structured approach works for any decision where you need to explore options, evaluate trade-offs, and get alignment. The principles apply broadly.
